sentences.base_language: Jisoo and I have been friends for ten years.
sentences.target_language: 지수랑 저는 친구가 된 지 10년이 됐어요.
sentences.romanized: Jisurang jeoneun chinguga doen ji sip nyeoni dwaesseoyo.
sentences.literal: Jisoo-with I[topic] friend-become[since] ten years became.
sentences.grammarNote: V-(으)ㄴ 지 + time + 되다/지나다 expresses how long it has been since something happened: 친구가 된 지 10년이 됐어요 = 'it's been 10 years since we became friends'. This is one of Korean's key patterns for talking about elapsed time.

**때** (ttae) = 'time / when / during' — used after nouns or verb modifier forms: 중학교 때 (during middle school), 어릴 때 (when young), 먹을 때 (when eating).
**중학교** (junghakgyo) = middle school (grades 7–9) — 중 (中, middle) + 학교 (school). Korea's school system: 초등학교 (elementary), 중학교 (middle), 고등학교 (high school).
**만나다** (mannada) = 'to meet' — polite past: 만났어요. One of the most fundamental verbs for social interaction.

sentences.base_language: We used to eat lunch together every day.
sentences.grammarNote: V-곤 했다 expresses a habitual past action: 먹곤 했어요 = 'used to eat'. It describes something that was a regular practice in the past but may no longer be. Compare with V-았/었어요 which is simply the past tense with no habitual implication.
Verb stem + -게 하다 means 'to make someone do' or 'to cause': 웃게 하다 (make laugh), 울게 하다 (make cry), 기다리게 하다 (make wait). This causative pattern is common in conversation.

sentences.base_language: She understands me better than anyone.
sentences.grammarNote:
**보다** (boda) = 'than' as a comparison particle, attached directly to the noun: 누구보다 (than anyone), 어제보다 (more than yesterday).
**그 누구보다** = 'more than any person / better than anyone' — 그 (that) + 누구 (anyone) + 보다 (than). An emphatic construction.
**이해하다** (ihaehada) = 'to understand' — 이해 (理解) + 하다. The object marker 를 follows 저 (me): 저를 이해하다 = 'to understand me.'

sentences.base_language: When I'm having a hard time, she is always there.
sentences.target_language: 제가 힘들 때 항상 곁에 있어 줘요.
sentences.romanized: Jega himdeul ttae hangsang gyeote isseo jweoyo.
sentences.literal: I hard-time when always side[at] be give.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-ㄹ/을 때** (ttae) = 'when ~': **힘들 때** = 'when it's hard'. **때** (時) is a Sino-Korean noun meaning 'time/moment' — attaches to a verb stem.
**곁에** (gyeote) = 'at one's side': **곁** = side + **에** location particle.
**있어 줘요** = **있어 주다** (isseo juda) in polite form — 'to be there for someone'. **-아/어 주다** adds the nuance of doing something for another person's benefit.

sentences.base_language: We don't need to talk to be together.
sentences.target_language: 함께 있기 위해 말할 필요가 없어요.
sentences.romanized: Hamkke itgi wihae malhal piryoga eopseoyo.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-기 위해(서)** (-gi wihae(seo)) = 'in order to / for the purpose of': 있기 위해 = 'in order to be together.'
**필요가 없다** (piryoga eopda) = 'there is no need to' — literally 'need does not exist.' The opposite: 필요가 있다 (there is a need).
**말하다** (malhada) = 'to speak / talk' — the modifier form 말할 before 필요 means 'the need to speak.'
함께 (hamkke) = together (formal) — the casual equivalent is 같이 (gachi).

sentences.base_language: Sometimes we just sit quietly and drink tea.
sentences.target_language: 가끔 그냥 조용히 앉아서 차를 마셔요.
sentences.romanized: Gakkeum geunyang joyonghi anjaseo chareul masyeoyo.
sentences.literal: Sometimes just quietly sit-and tea[obj] drink.
sentences.grammarNote:
**그냥** (geunyang) = 'just / simply / without particular reason' — adds a casual, effortless feeling to the action.
**조용히** (joyonghi) = 'quietly' — adverb form of 조용하다 (to be quiet), formed with -히. Other -히 adverbs: 열심히 (diligently), 솔직히 (honestly).
**앉아서** uses -아서/어서 to sequence: 'sit down and (then) drink' — the sitting is the position from which the second action happens.
**차** (cha) = tea — a native Korean word for all types of tea.

sentences.base_language: I am so grateful to have her in my life.
sentences.target_language: 그녀가 내 삶에 있어서 정말 감사해요.
sentences.romanized: Geunyeoga nae srame isseo-seo jeongmal gamsahaeyo.
sentences.literal: She[subj] my life[at] exist-because really thankful.
sentences.grammarNote: -아서/어서 can express reason: 있어서 감사해요 = 'thankful because (she) is here'. In emotional contexts, Koreans often express gratitude by stating the reason first and ending with 감사해요 or 고마워요.

**진정한** (jinjeonghaneun) = true/genuine: **진정** (眞正, jinjeong) is Sino-Korean — **진** (眞) = truth + **정** (正) = correct. The **-한** ending makes it an attributive adjective.
**우정** (友情, ujeong) = friendship: **우** (友) = friend + **정** (情) = feeling/bond. Compare: **애정** (愛情, love), **동정** (同情, sympathy).
**보물이에요** = 'is a treasure': **보물** (寶物, bomul) + **이에요** copula. **보** (寶) = treasure + **물** (物) = thing.
